summarization.a: The 2θχ XRD patterns of 100cLi–MnO₂, 200cLi–MnO₂, and 300cLi–MnO₂ show dominant reflections indexed to the lithiated spinel-like phase Li₁−xMn₂O₄, including the (111), (131), and (440) planes at ~18°, ~36–37°, and ~65–66°, respectively. With increasing cycle count, these Li₁−xMn₂O₄ peaks remain clearly resolved and comparable in relative intensity.
summarization.b: The θ–2θ XRD patterns highlight surface-normal diffraction features of the same samples, with pronounced “lithiated phase” peaks appearing near ~18° and ~35°, along with Li₁−xMn₂O₄ (400) reflections near ~44.5°. Residual β-MnO₂ is observed through the (200) reflection around ~40–41°, most clearly in the 100cLi–MnO₂ sample.

| 300cLi–MnO₂ | Li₁−xMn₂O₄ (440) | ~65–66 |

a.question: What structural implication arises from observing Li₁−xMn₂O₄ reflections together with residual β-MnO₂ peaks in the 2θχ scan?
a.answer: The coexistence of Li₁−xMn₂O₄ and β-MnO₂ reflections indicates incomplete or spatially heterogeneous lithiation. This suggests that some regions of the material have transformed into the lithiated spinel-like phase, while others retain the original β-MnO₂ structure. Such phase coexistence points to limitations in lithium penetration, reaction kinetics, or structural accessibility during cycling.

a.question: Why is it important to monitor the presence of the Li₂−zMnO₂ impurity phase in XRD patterns?
a.answer:
-Impurity phases can reduce electrochemically active material fraction.
-They may introduce structural instability during cycling.
-Phase impurities can affect capacity retention and voltage behavior.
-Identifying impurities helps optimize processing and cycling conditions.

b.question: Does a θ–2θ scan primarily report diffraction from lattice planes oriented parallel to the sample surface?
Which labeled peaks indicate the “lithiated phase” in the θ–2θ patterns, and where do they appear?
